metadata.title: Shortest path-based centrality metrics in attributed graphs with node-individual context constraints

abstract:
Centrality measurements are a well-known method to assess the importance of actors in networks. They are easy to obtain and provide a versatile interpretability adaptable to the meaning of nodes and edges. The current centrality measurements use structural information alone. In real-world situations, however, actors and the connections between them are subject to contextual settings and can be significantly influenced by these settings.

In fact, such real-world observations are often modeled using attributed networks in which contextual information can be associated as attributes to nodes and edges. However, this information is disregarded when evaluating the importance of actors in terms of network centrality measurements. Hence, this paper proposes a method for obtaining shortest path-based centrality measurements for attributed networks that exploit attribute information on nodes for shortest path calculations. We add abstracts of scientific publications to a co-publishing network and use topic models to create node-individual context constraints for shortest path calculations. This creates additional analytic opportunities and can aid in gaining a detailed understanding of complex social networks.

The property which makes one path between two regions of the life space outstanding seems to vary considerably with the situation. Sometimes the fastest connection is outstanding; at other times it is the cheapest connection, or the most pleasant, or the least dangerous.

paragraphs: During the early years of social network analysis, numerous variants of centrality measurements were proposed (Shimbel, 1953;Anthonisse, 1971;Freeman, 1979). One of the earliest approaches considering node attributes for measuring centrality was proposed by Everett and Borgatti much later (Everett & Borgatti, 2012).

In this paper, we consider node attributes to be the context information about nodes expressing certain individual interests. In addition, we consider these interests to influence the perspective that individual nodes have on their surroundings; that is, because of similar interests, one might see a connection to a distant peer, and at the same time, differing interests might lead to disregarding peers with whom one shares a direct relation. This node-individual perspective is modeled by obtaining individual sets of shortest paths, or geodesics, from which centrality values are then derived. In sampling unique sets of shortest paths, this study fundamentally differs from previous studies on centrality metrics.

Variants of betweenness centrality The betweenness concept was first used by Shimbel (1953) to calculate values for each node in a given network. Shimbel's stress centrality CS indicates that if a node n a is located at an intermediate position between two other nodes n i and n j , then n a has a "certain responsibility" toward n i and n j . This results in stress if there is activity in the network. CS calculates the shortest paths from each node to every other node and counts the number of shortest paths, including node n a .

Later, Anthonisse (1971) defined the rush of a node as the total flow through a node on shortest paths. Freeman (1979) connected the probability of this in-between position with the notion of information control. These network positions are also responsible for concatenating different parts of a network (Burt, 2005). In his much noticed work, Freeman (1979) defined three basic centrality measurementsdegree, closeness, and betweenness centralitywith the latter two being based on shortest path calculations and all of these metrics ignoring the attributes of nodes and edges.

Several approaches that extend the notion of centrality measurements to propose different methods of weighting individual shortest paths while obtaining centrality values have been proposed. For example, Brandes (2008) compared several variants of shortest path-based betweenness centrality, some of which apply weights to shortest paths. The surveyed algorithms consider a globally valid set of geodesics and derive weights from structural information alone.

One such example is the length-scaled betweenness proposed by Borgatti & Everett (2006). Here, the longer the shortest paths, the lesser is the weight assigned to them, based on the idea that the longer a shortest path, the less valuable it might be to control this path. A similar approach was proposed by Ghosh & Lerman (2011) with the normalized alpha-centrality including the total number of paths from a node exponentially attenuated by their length. Geisberger et al. (2008) proposed linearly-scaled betweenness using a distance function to weigh a path based on its relative distance from a source node.

Of these and further variants, which were discussed in detail by Brandes (2008), with the only exception being the linearly scaled betweenness, none of the surveyed algorithms considers a node-individual weight for the set of geodesics.

Another approach originally aiming at an improved calculation of betweenness centrality was proposed by Pfeffer & Carley (2012). These authors elaborated on the idea of bounded-distance shortest path calculations; that is, the maximum length of shortest paths is fixed, which effectively speeds up graph traversal. This idea can be exploited to model nodes' individual views on the network; however, this node-individual view is not influenced by any context information. For the last variant, we mention Zhang et al. (2015), who proposed a new centrality score as a linear combination of betweenness and Katz centrality. This allows all network paths to have a certain influence on the centrality score according to the definition of Katz centrality (Katz, 1953). However, it neither considers context information nor assigns node-individual weights to paths.
All abovementioned variants neither consider attributes on nodes nor on edges. They rather derive weights for shortest paths from the paths themselves or other relational information.

One of the earliest approaches that considered node attributes for measuring centrality was proposed by Everett and Borgatti much later (Everett & Borgatti, 2012). Their node attributes are of categorical nature, partitioning the graph into multiple groups with and without a certain attribute. One part of their concept is based on the E-I index, which expresses homophily by Krackhardt & Stern (1988). This E-I index is exploited as a partitioning degree centrality and extended to a closeness and eigenvector centrality (Bonacich, 1972). The other part is based on the Gould and Fernandez brokerage measure (Gould & Fernandez, 1989), which, as the authors showed, can be generalized to the whole network, effectively yielding betweenness centrality.

A more recent approach was proposed by Benyahia & Largeron (2015), which also considers weighted attributed networks with numerical attributes on nodes. However, this approach uses node attributes to weigh centrality values and lacks a node-individual view on the network.

Multilayer Networks. If there are multiple views on a network or there is more than one set of geodesics, the use of multilayer or multiplex networks can be considered. Such networks are a particular type of complex networks in which each node is represented on multiple layers and each layer comprises a specific set of edges. Multilayer networks contain multiple types of relations, which are represented on different layers. The number of these different types of relations decides on the number of layers. Multilayer networks aim to model multiple types of relations that connect the same set of nodes (De Domenico et al., 2013;Kivelä et al., 2014;Boccaletti et al., 2014). Examples of these networks include networks between people who have friendly relations, family bonds, and acquaintances. Each type of relation occupies a distinct layer that connects the same people through various patterns. Another common example is a network of airports that are connected through different airlines. Each layer of connections contains all flight connections of one particular airline.

Among the studies conducted on multilayer networks, those characterizing the structure using centrality metrics and node ranking form only a small fraction. A much larger part of recent research activities has been devoted to analyzing the percolation, resilience, diffusion, and other processes in multilayer networks (Boccaletti et al., 2014).

However, most of the studies conducted on centrality metrics have focused on a specific interplay between layers. For example, De Domenico et al. (2015) proposed a metric that aims at finding the nodes that play central roles in the cohesion of the entire structure, bridging various relations. These nodes are considered the most versatile in a multilayer network and hence receive a higher ranking.

Another study assumed that the centrality a node has in one layer affects the centrality it can obtain in another layer. Building on this, the authors proposed a set of centrality metrics based on the PageRank algorithm (Page et al., 1999), which allows a random walker to jump between layers either contributing to the node's importance in another layer, deriving benefits from the importance of other nodes that point to it in another layer, or a combination of both (Halu et al., 2013).

Similar approaches were proposed by Iacovacci et al. (2016), Solá et al. (2013), Kumar et al. (2019), andPedroche et al. (2019), all of which adopted a common approach for identifying a smart use of different layers and a combination thereof.

However, the type of attributed networks considered in this study are often not translatable into a multilayer representation. This is because we focus on attributed networks that denote a single type of relation only. This means that the relations themselves cannot be split into distinct layers or dimensions. The only way to identify various relations is to segregate the network based on the attribute information. However, we focus on attribute data that are multivariate or complex themselves. Hence, such contextual information would neither facilitate an evident segregation nor produce a comprehensible multilayer view.

Contributions. By evaluating the attribute information during shortest path discovery, we operationalize the notion of individual outreach within certain contexts. This means that we account for the fact that each node has a unique view on its surroundings, which may be subject to individual contextual constraints. A node's unique view on the network is represented by a set of shortest paths that corresponds to the node's attributes and implied contextual constraints.

Considering the context in which nodes and connecting paths are embedded accounts for the fact that social networks occur in different cultural, institutional, or material settings, which affect tie formation processes, stabilization of social relations, and ultimately, network topology. Letting individual contexts influence the discovery of paths and the calculation of centrality values serves as a model to study a network's ecology; that is, we focus on the relation between individual environmental settings and the resulting perceptions of the network.

We obtain the node-individual view on the network by comparing the attribute information of one node with that of all other nodes in the network, and accordingly, weight the corresponding shortest paths, allowing zero weights, which effectively prohibits certain paths. This results in a set of shortest paths that represents the view of only a single node rather than all geodesics of the entire network.

Based on this understanding of geodesics, we build upon the idea of Kurt Lewin (Lewin, 1938), who was a pioneer of social network analysis. He stated that "one cannot use shortness as a general principle" to select geodesics (see quote at the beginning of the article). Other studies building upon on Lewin's idea, discussing shortest path characteristics, explicitly excluded the dynamic factors in the problem of defining a shortest path and rather focused on "purely positional" criteria to be able to "develop mathematical statements of distance and direction" (Bavelas, 1948). This line of thought has prevailed, and up to now, shortest paths are considered to be precisely defined by a network structure alone.

Allowing a certain dynamic to steer the identification of shortest paths has immediate consequences for centrality metrics considering shortest paths and shortest path distances, especially closeness and betweenness centrality. While extracting an individual's view on the network before obtaining their centrality values, we effectively select a set of geodesics that is valid for the calculation of this particular individual's centrality value only. This is contrary to, for example, Freeman's clarifications of centrality values, which imply that all possible geodesics count and all geodesics are equally important for all nodes. He defined that "a point that falls on the communication paths between other points exhibits a potential for control of their communication", which is expressed in terms of betweenness centrality, or as a measure of closeness, "a point is central to the degree that the distances associated with all its geodesics are minimum" (Freeman, 1979, p.225). In summary, this paper makes the following contributions to the literature:

• Shortest path calculation in attributed graphs that considers attribute information on nodes • Adaptation of betweenness centrality and closeness centrality metrics using values of their classic counterparts as upper bounds • Offering an understanding of node embeddedness into the contextual settings of their peers

The remainder of this paper is organized as follows. Section 2 presents our concept in detail. Section 3 describes our data. Section 4 presents our results. Section 5 discusses our approach in comparison with other related studies. Section 6 concludes the paper.

sections.paper_id: Schoenfeld and Pfeffer - 2024 - Shortest path-based centrality metrics in attributed graphs with node-individual context constraints
paragraphs: We consider a network as a graph G = (V, E) comprising a set of vertices V and a set of connecting edges E ⫅ V × V. For an edge e = (u, v) ∈ E, u is called the head of the edge, v is called its tail, and u is adjacent to v.

A path P (s, t) from a source s ∈ V to a target t ∈ V is an alternating sequence of vertices and edges s, (s, v 1 ), v 1 , (v 1 , v 2 ), v 2 , …, (v k , t), t starting at s and ending at t. We denote by V P (s, t) a set of vertices along this path, including its source s and target t. The length of a path is the number of edges it contains and the distance d (s, t) from s to t is defined as the minimum length of any path between s and t, if one exists; if no such path exists, d(s, t) remains undefined. For s = t, d(s, t) = 0.

Attributed networks contain contextual information related to vertices and/or edges of the underlying graph. However, we focus on attributed networks with contextual information present on vertices only. Hence, the above definitions of networks and structural characteristics are extended such that an attributed network G a = (V, E, V ctx ) includes a set of attribute information on nodes V ctx with |V ctx | = |V|. Furthermore, we denote d ctx (u, v) yielding the contextual distance as the difference between the vertex attributes of u and v. Generally, this distance function must fit the type of vertex attributes and can be any distance metric that fulfills the triangle inequality.

A path under context constraints P ctx (s, t, u, ϵ) is defined as an alternating sequence of vertices and edges in which all v i , t ∈ V show a contextual divergence not higher than a pre-defined threshold ϵ from a vertex u such that d ctx (u, v i )≤ϵ, which includes d ctx (u, t)≤ϵ. The vertex u, which induces the context constraints by its associated set of attributes, might be the source or target of the path; hence, u = s or u = t. However, this is not a requirement. Instead, vertex u ∈ V might be any vertex of the network and might not even be part of any shortest path between s and t. The resulting set of vertices V Pctx (s, t, u, ϵ) along a constraint path is given by

Accordingly, σ ctx (s, t, u, ϵ) denotes the number of shortest paths under the context constraints induced by the attributes of node u, and σ ctx (s, t, u, ϵ|u) denotes that under the context constraints that pass through u. Note that, according to our definition of P ctx (s, t, u, ϵ), the contextual constraints induced by vertex u will most likely influence remote parts of the network that might not be directly connected to vertex u. This allows us to model the individual view of vertex u on the network. Fig. 1 depicts the resulting view of vertex u on its surroundings depending on the strictness of the constraints. This vertex is highlighted in all network visualizations.

In the figure, we assume to have used a network of acquaintances. The vertices have their age in years associated as a contextual attribute. We define a distance function d ctx (u, v) = |age(u) -age(v)|≤ϵ such that it indicates whether the age difference is within certain bounds. Using this distance function during path traversal provides us with acquaintances that are in the same age range as the target node. In Fig. 1, we assume that the four sub-networks are produced using thresholds ϵ = 5, ϵ = 7, ϵ = 10, and ϵ = 25 years and the target node is vertex u, which is highlighted in all four visualizations. Fig. 1 a shows that a strict boundary for an age range induces a small part of the network to be included in u's view. Relaxing the constraints leads to a gradual growth of u's view, as shown in Fig. 1b. The difference between Figs. 1a and 1b indicates how a remote part of the network is influenced by u's constraints. Consider a path from s to t that does not involve u. Under the enforcement of strict constraints, this path is not included in u's view (Fig. 1a). However, under slightly relaxed constraints, a path to t becomes available (Fig. 1b). Further relaxations lead to more parts of the network being included in u's view (Fig. 1c) until the individual view spans the entire network (Fig. 1d) due to negligible constraints. An age range of 25 years does not exclude any of u's acquaintances.

Another example for the network in Fig. 1 is a network of hyperlinked websites in which the nodes represent websites and edges represent links between these websites. The node attributes describe the text of the pages and language models derived from it. We define a distance function d ctx (u, v), which compares these models. A strict constraint on this comparison yielding the leftmost network in Fig. 1a highlights websites that use a common and highly specialized vocabulary similar to the vocabulary of website u. The strictness of constraints (i.e., the specialization of vocabularies) even leads to the path from s to t being excluded from u's view.

In this study, we analyze a network of co-authorships where contextual constraints are implied by research interests of individuals. The resulting view of an individual on such a network of scholars contains only those collaborations that are related to an individual's interest to a certain extent. Consequently, collaborations (i.e., edges) without a minimal relation to an individual's interests are hidden from a contextually constrained view.

These examples illustrate that the distance function is responsible for constructing individual perspectives on the network. Deciding on an appropriate way of determining whether nodes are within the reach of others is a crucial modeling decision. In terms of network ecology, which refers to focusing on the interplay between the network structure and contextual conditions of the structure, the distance function is where this interplay is represented and modeled.

Depending on the available data, the distance function must be able to deal with sparse data. The researcher needs to model the case in which node attributes might be missing, as this noticeably impacts the resulting set V Pctx . Moreover, depending on the scenario, the distance function might need to be able to handle heterogeneous data (e.g., where nodes have different types of attributes assigned).

As a final remark on the choice of d ctx , note that this function is evaluated at each step of path traversal. Therefore, performing heavy calculations inside d ctx is not recommended, as it will slow down the path discovery significantly.

sections.paper_id: Schoenfeld and Pfeffer - 2024 - Shortest path-based centrality metrics in attributed graphs with node-individual context constraints
paragraphs: Both centrality metrics are implemented using the Boost Graph Library (Siek et al., 2002). This C+ + library ships with an efficient implementation of Dijkstra's algorithm for shortest path calculations (Dijkstra, 1959). It allows for extending the execution of Dijkstra's algorithm by the so-called visitors. A visitor is informed whether a visited edge is added to a shortest path.

For our implementation of closeness centrality, we provide a custom visitor that evaluates d ctx (u, v i )≤ϵ for each edge that is reported as being part of a shortest path within s, t ∈ V. If a constraint is exceeded, the corresponding edge is removed from the path and the distance updates are rolled back.

For our implementation of betweenness centrality, we modify the visitor that is used as part of the library's implementation of Brandes' algorithm for betweenness centrality (Brandes, 2001). Then, we count how often a constraint d ctx (u, v i )≤ϵ is met along a path connecting s, t ∈ V.

The resulting algorithms have been published as the programming library nctx, which is available for both Rfoot_0 and Python. 4 The library leaves the specification of d ctx (u, v) to the user but evaluates it at each step during path traversal.

sections.paper_id: Schoenfeld and Pfeffer - 2024 - Shortest path-based centrality metrics in attributed graphs with node-individual context constraints
paragraphs: The example we illustrate throughout this paper is a co-authorship network of scientific publications. In this network, nodes represent scholars and edges indicate common papers. As context information, we use a topic model of the underlying publications. The topic assignments of individual documents are aggregated and associated with the node network. Therefore, this network allows us to grasp the importance and impact of individual researchers by evaluating their co-authorship connections and contrast this picture with the data on their specialization and diversification with respect to certain fields of research.

In this study, we combine data on scientific collaborations with those on the content of these collaborations. We use a dataset obtained from arxiv.org, which contains metadata for 41 000 papers published on the online database between 1992 and 2018. As this platform is often used by the STEM community, we obtain papers related to the fields of machine learning, artificial intelligence, and computer vision. The metadata of the articles cover information on the date of publication, involved authors, and abstract of the publication. We derive our network from the authorship information and contextual attributes from the abstracts.

Therefore, this network allows us to grasp the importance and impact of individual researchers by evaluating their co-authorship connections and contrast this picture with the data on their specialization and diversification with respect to certain fields of research.

In this study, we combine data on scientific collaborations with those on the content of these collaborations. We use a dataset obtained from arxiv.org, which contains metadata for 41 000 papers published on the online database between 1992 and 2018. As this platform is often used by the STEM community, we obtain papers related to the fields of machine learning, artificial intelligence, and computer vision. The metadata of the articles cover information on the date of publication, involved authors, and abstract of the publication. We derive our network from the authorship information and contextual attributes from the abstracts.

sections.paper_id: Schoenfeld and Pfeffer - 2024 - Shortest path-based centrality metrics in attributed graphs with node-individual context constraints
paragraphs: For illustrative purposes, we extract a small network for further analysis. The construction of the network is fairly straightforward. From the dataset, we randomly choose the year 2015 and obtain 4 467 papers published in that particular year. For these papers, we collect all authors and filter out those appearing less than three times. For each of the remaining scholars, we instantiate a vertex. Edges connect vertices who have co-published at least one paper together. The number of publications is ignored (i.e., all edges have the same weight of 1). At this point, the network comprises 951 vertices and 1 085 edges. To ensure a connected network for calculating the closeness centrality, we select the largest component and discard all remaining vertices and edges. This yields a network of 22 vertices connected through 60 edges. The remaining network has an edge density of ~ 0.26, a clustering coefficient of ~ 0.612, and a diameter of 3. Each node has an average degree of ~ 5.455. The network is depicted in Fig. 1.

sections.header_raw: From text to context information: topic modeling
sections.header_norm: from text to context information topic modeling
paragraphs: To associate network information with relevant attribute data, we select the content of co-publishing activity; that is, we use the abstracts of publications to obtain a topic model. From this topic model, we derive contextual attribute information for the network vertices.

The topic model is calculated using the entire dataset of 41 000 publication abstracts. This ensures that the model incorporates the entire set of papers and is not biased against any research direction by sub-setting the database.

There are numerous algorithms available to obtain a topic model of the text data. The most well-known algorithm is Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA), proposed by Blei et al. (2003). This algorithm serves as a basis for several current topic modeling procedures, which are often extensions or tailored modifications of the classical approach. We decide to adopt one such extensionstructural topic models (STM) (Roberts et al., 2014(Roberts et al., ,2013)). Its main advantage over the classical LDA is that it considers metadata as covariates for the detection of topics in documents. We do not consider metadata for documents but rather exploit the fact that there exists an efficient implementation of STM that incorporates moment-based estimators to initialize the modeling parameters (Arora et al., 2013).

Before modeling topics, we pre-process the text data of our corpus (i. e., lower-casing words, removal of punctuation, removal of common stopwords, removal of frequent words, and removal of seldom words). We exclude words that occur less than five times in the corpus and in more than 40% of all abstracts. We also remove words with one or two characters. We apply lemmatization to a set of documents by replacing each inflected form of words with their base form using a dictionary. Finally, the frequently collocated tokens are combined, ensuring that common (technical) terms serve as single features in the topic modeling procedure. To be counted as a compound token, the tokens must cooccur at least 10 times in the corpus.

Similar to the classical LDA, the STM procedure does not select a definite number of topics. Thus, we use both semantic coherence and exclusivity of topic descriptions to assess the quality of a given topic model. Semantic coherence of topics is described by Mimno et al. (2011) and is closely related to the pointwise mutual information of topic descriptions as it is maximized when the most probable words frequently co-occur in a topic. In contrast, the exclusivity of topic descriptions is described by Bischof & Airoldi (2012) and is simply the exclusivity of words in topics. By iteratively executing STMs, varying the number of topics for each iteration, and calculating the abovementioned metric, a number of topics can be chosen, which yields a topic model with maximal expressiveness in terms of both information divergence and quality. We follow this iterative approach by varying the number of topics k to consider the values between 5 and 50. The results suggest that k = 23 is a reasonable trade-off between the number of topics and their quality. Thus, we fix the number of topics to k = 23.

The result of topic modeling is a N × k matrix θ, which expresses the assignments of each of the N documents of the corpus to each of the k topics of the topic model. The assignments are expressed in terms of probabilities: a document is associated with a non-negative probability that each k topic will appear in that particular document. The sum of the assignment probabilities of a document (i.e., the sum of vectors of length k) is 1.0.
As each document is also assigned one or more authors, we can construct a vector of topic assignments for each scholar in the dataset. Thus, a scholar's s topic assignments are obtained as

where θ s is the number of relevant rows in the topic assignment matrix θ containing topic assignments for all documents of the given scholar and n s = |θ s | is the number of documents published by the given scholar.

Thus, t(s) represents a vector of length k containing mean assignments to each of the k topics. This procedure is followed for all scholars present in the example network used throughout the following section.

sections.paper_id: Schoenfeld and Pfeffer - 2024 - Shortest path-based centrality metrics in attributed graphs with node-individual context constraints
paragraphs: We obtain both betweenness centrality and closeness centrality in the abovementioned attributed network of co-authorship, in which the topic assignments of authors (i.e., vectors of length k) are assigned as attribute information to nodes. In this section, we evaluate the influence of attribute information on centrality rankings. This is enabled by our approach in two ways. First, we can use a complete vector of topic assignments t(s) for an individual scholar s to measure its influence on centrality ranking. This leads to comparisons between two such vectors during path traversal. Consequently, we need to choose an appropriate distance measure that supports a comparison of the two vectors. We decide to use the Kullback-Leibler divergence (KL divergence), which expresses the difference between two probability distributions P and Q (Kullback & Leibler, 1951):
) which defines d ctx (u, v) as follows:

where P = t(u) is the probability distribution induced by u's topic assignments and Q = t(v) is that induced by v's topic assignments. KL divergence does not satisfy the triangle inequality, and hence, is not a metric (i.e., d ctx (u, v) ‡ d ctx (v, u)). As a result, an edge connecting u and v might not be considered for all path discoveries and a set of geodesics can be truly individual for each node: obtaining the centrality for u means evaluating d ctx (u, v) at some point. Let us assume that the constraints are fulfilled for this direction and the edge between u and v is traversed. Calculating the centrality for v means evaluating d ctx (v, u) on the same edge as before. In the absence of the triangle inequality, this might yield a different result than d ctx (u, v) and the edge might not be considered for v's view on the network. Using such a "pseudo"-metric allows us to model an individual perception of similarity of research interests, which means that one scholar u might consider their research interest to be similar to that of v but v might think differently.

The second possibility of measuring the influence of attribute information on centrality rankings is to obtain centrality values for each topic (i.e., the metric d ctx is exchanged such that it compares the aggregated assignments for single topics):

where i represents the current topic of interest. This distance function calculates the relative difference between two aggregated topic assignments such that it does not satisfy the triangle inequality either (i.e., d ctx (u, v) ‡ d ctx (v, u)). As mentioned above, this is used to incorporate a truly node-individual view on the network. By obtaining centrality values for each topic, we can assess the influence of individual topics on the centrality of a node by accounting for the following questions: In which topics do nodes attain higher or lower centrality values? In which topics is a node's centrality value rather stable for an increasing ϵ and in which topics is it rather volatile? Where do we see "winners" or "losers" -centrality-wise? Similar questions can be posed to a multilayer network in which each topic is represented by an individual layer and edges are added to a layer only if it is allowed in terms of the distance function d ctx (u, v) and a fixed threshold ϵ. If a node-individual view should be compiled as in our approach, where the result of comparing the node attributes depends on the view (i.e., the reference node u), it would require |V| × k different layers.
For both variants, we vary the threshold ϵ in 2000 steps such that the interval [0.01,max(d ctx (u, v)] is evenly represented.

By considering the variation in ϵ as a form of development, we focus on the development of topic-dependent centrality values. This is because the meaning of a single threshold value might be difficult to interpret. Putting the single values into context allows us to assess the "embeddedness" of nodes into their respective surroundings. Thus, we can determine whether individuals are sole specialists among their peers or are well-integrated into their peers' overall interests.

Aiming at understanding the node embeddedness, we need to evaluate each node's individual topic-dependent perspective on the network and repeat this evaluation for several different values of ϵ. At this point, the existing methodology of multilayer networks falls short as the number of layers is |V| × k for each value of ϵ; that is, we will take up |V| × k × 2000 layers.
This section first highlights our experiments on context-specific betweenness centrality and continues with those on closeness centrality.

paragraphs: We begin with the definition of d ctx given in Equation 7. This allows us to obtain betweenness centrality by comparing the overall research interests. By repeating the calculation of betweenness with an increasing threshold ϵ, we evaluate different constraints for path traversal.
Data: The overall research interests are represented as a complete vector of topic assignments t(s) for a scholar s. Consequently, as a distance function, we use d ctx as specified in Equation 7.

Findings:Fig. 2 depicts the mean betweenness values gained by the vertices of our example network (see Section 3.1 for a network description). The mean value aggregates all different constellations of ϵ. Our example network comprises five vertices that attract certain betweenness ratings.

The error bars in Fig. 2 depict the standard deviation. The overlapping error bars indicate that constraining path traversal and obtaining constrained betweenness centrality values can perturb the node ranking compared to their ranking induced by the classic betweenness centrality. This will be further discussed in the following section in relation to Fig. 3.

The resulting centrality values and their variances depend heavily on the chosen distance function d ctx as this distance function determines the set of shortest paths. Using a different notion of dissimilarity here can produce a different histogram of centrality scores in which even the ranking might be completely perturbed. This is further investigated in the following section.

We begin with the definition of d ctx given in Equation 7. This allows us to obtain betweenness centrality by comparing the overall research interests. By repeating the calculation of betweenness with an increasing threshold ϵ, we evaluate different constraints for path traversal.

Data: The overall research interests are represented as a complete vector of topic assignments t(s) for a scholar s. Consequently, as a distance function, we use d ctx as specified in Equation 7.

Findings:Fig. 2 depicts the mean betweenness values gained by the vertices of our example network (see Section 3.1 for a network description). The mean value aggregates all different constellations of ϵ. Our example network comprises five vertices that attract certain betweenness ratings.

The error bars in Fig. 2 depict the standard deviation. The overlapping error bars indicate that constraining path traversal and obtaining constrained betweenness centrality values can perturb the node ranking compared to their ranking induced by the classic betweenness centrality. This will be further discussed in the following section in relation to Fig. 3.

The resulting centrality values and their variances depend heavily on the chosen distance function d ctx as this distance function determines the set of shortest paths. Using a different notion of dissimilarity here can produce a different histogram of centrality scores in which even the ranking might be completely perturbed. This is further investigated in the following section.

sections.paper_id: Schoenfeld and Pfeffer - 2024 - Shortest path-based centrality metrics in attributed graphs with node-individual context constraints
paragraphs: As a next step, we evaluate betweenness centrality using the distance function from Equation 8. This allows us to obtain betweenness centrality for individual topics. By repeating the calculation of betweenness with an increasing threshold ϵ, we evaluate the development of betweenness values in detail.

Data: The individual research topics are expressed by single entries to the vector of topic assignments t(s) for a scholar s; that is, their research interests in topic i are given by t(s) [i]. Consequently, as a distance function, we use d ctx , as specified in Equation 8.

Findings: We begin by inspecting the amount of perturbation of the rankings. As shown in Fig. 3, the extent to which rankings are changed differs between topics. To calculate this extent, we first obtain a ranking from the classic betweenness centrality by considering the structural information alone. This ranking serves as a baseline for those induced by the constrained betweenness centrality.

To obtain a ranking under context constraints for an individual topic, we fix the threshold ϵ, and accordingly, calculate the betweenness values by considering assignments of a topic of interest only. Between the resulting ranking and baseline ranking, we calculate the Kendall tau metric, which efficiently counts the number of operations required to convert one ranking into another (Kendall, 1938).
By iteratively incrementing the threshold ϵ, as described above, we obtain a distribution of Kendall tau distances for each topic, that is depicted in Fig. 3 by its mean and standard deviation.

The figure shows that the extent of perturbation varies considerably between topics. Topics 13 and 19, for example, indicate a high mean Kendall tau distance, meaning that rankings deviate noticeably from the baseline ranking. The standard deviation emphasizes that this is the case for many ϵ values. These topics apparently induce constraints that have extensive effects on path traversal and shortest path calculations.

In contrast, in Topic 17, we observe only a slight deviation between the constraint and baseline rankings, as well as a narrow standard deviation. Here, the constraints induced by the topic do not appear to have large effects on shortest path calculations.

This section shows that we can obtain a different view on the network by simply exchanging d ctx . In the previous section 4.1.1, we used a function that considers the whole vector of topic assignments t(v).
Here, focusing on single entries of this vector opens up another perspective.

sections.paper_id: Schoenfeld and Pfeffer - 2024 - Shortest path-based centrality metrics in attributed graphs with node-individual context constraints
paragraphs: We further inspect the influence of context information on rankings by analyzing how individual nodes benefit, in that whether they gain higher ranks or forfeit.

Data: As in Section 4.1.2, we work with individual entries to the topic vector t(s) here; that is, we obtain betweenness centrality for a topic i by using t(s) [i]. Consequently, as a distance function, we use d ctx as specified in Equation 8. We repeat the evaluation of betweenness centrality for a varying threshold ϵ. Findings:Fig. 4 shows two example nodes representing RS(colored gray) and JB(colored orange). The graphs depict how often a certain rank is observed in a particular topic using violin plots. The figure highlights ranks 1-7 to support readability.

The figure assumes that the classic betweenness centrality values of each node serve as an upper bound to the constraint betweenness scores as σ ctx ≤ σ. The resulting ranking, however, might be perturbed as certain nodes might be associated with lower centrality scores than the others.

For example, in Topic 4, the node RSappears at position 1, which is shown by the wider upper part of the violin. However, at some point, the node also appears at the end of the ranking. The lower part of the violins have been skipped for the figure as they only report very few observations at the end of the rankings. For the same topic, the figure depicts that the node JBoften appears at position 5 but also at higher ranks. The horizontal bar indicates the rank obtained from classical betweenness centrality as an orientation, which is rank 1 for RS(gray bar) and rank 5 for JB(orange bar).

These graphs show two types of nodes. RS's node only forfeits as, in all topics, it appears at lower ranks than through classic betweenness without any exception. In contrast, JBoften benefits, as it appears at higher ranks in all topics.
Topic 13 appears as an interesting example in these graphs. Here, RSforfeits the mostthe violin shows accumulation below the gray barwhereas JBprimarily benefits.

As the graphs summarize the rankings for all values of ϵ, extracting a certain scenario means to fix ϵ to the corresponding value. The next section highlights a few scenarios with a fixed ϵ and outlines the effects on betweenness centrality scores.

sections.paper_id: Schoenfeld and Pfeffer - 2024 - Shortest path-based centrality metrics in attributed graphs with node-individual context constraints
paragraphs: At this point, it is worthwhile to investigate the relation between context information and betweenness values. Therefore, we select RSto serve as an example for further analysis and trace the development of the node's betweenness in relation to its aggregated topic assignments. We select this node because it is one of the nodes that gain some betweenness value (Fig. 2) as well as some closeness centrality (Fig. 7), which enables a better comparison with the evaluations of closeness centrality (see the following Section 4.2). It is also the highlighted node u in Fig. 1.

Data: At this point, we are working with individual entries to the topic vector t(s); that is, we obtain betweenness centrality for a topic i by using t(s) [i]. Consequently, as a distance function, we use d ctx as specified in Equation 8.

Findings: We obtain the betweenness centrality values for all variations of the topics and threshold values for ϵ. To highlight the development of centrality values, we extract values at 25%, 50%, 75%, and90% quantiles of the ϵ interval for each topic. The results are depicted in Fig. 5.

This figure gives an impression of the development of betweenness centrality values for an increasing value of ϵ and different constellations of topic assignments. The topic assignments are normalized by their maximum value to fit the interval [0,1].

Betweenness values are also depicted as relative values ranging within [0,1]. These values are relative to the classic betweenness values derived from structural information alone. This is possible because, by definition, the classic betweenness values serve as an upper bound to the constrained betweenness values (see Equation 3).

First, we select some noteworthy constellations. Topics 2 and 10, for example, show a rather weak normalized aggregated topic assignment. It shows how the centrality value quickly increases and gets quite close to the classic betweenness value in the rightmost figure, which corresponds to the highest value of ϵ in this comparison (i.e., the most relaxed constraints for d ctx ).
In Topic 15, we observe a medium aggregated topic assignment. Although the topic assignment is not very strong, centrality values start increasing with rather relaxed constraints only.

The examples for strong topic assignments can be observed in Topics 13 and 20. Centrality values remain quite low for an increasing value of ϵ. In Topic 20, the centrality values only reach approximately 25% of the classic structural betweenness value. In Topic 13, we observe a maximum relative betweenness of approximately 50%.

From the individual histograms as well as their sequence, we can learn about the node and its neighborhood; that is, we can assess if a node shares certain interests with its peers and to what extent are the strengths of the interests equal. Due to the formulation of d ctx (Equation 8), we focus on the gap between the topic assignments.

In topics in which the relative betweenness centrality remains low even for relaxed constraints, the gap between the topic assignments of the nodes and peers is expected to be rather high as many edges have been forbidden during the collection of shortest paths. In contrast, the gap between the topic assignments is rather low for topics in which the relative betweenness centrality quickly approaches unconstrained values even for tighter constraints.
From Fig. 5, we can ultimately learn about the embeddedness of a node into the contextual settings of its peers. We elaborate on this in the following section.

sections.paper_id: Schoenfeld and Pfeffer - 2024 - Shortest path-based centrality metrics in attributed graphs with node-individual context constraints
paragraphs: Through Fig. 6, we provide a visual translation of the embeddedness of nodes as the Herfindahl-Hirshman index of the barplots of betweenness values. Hirschman (1945) originally proposed this index as an economic concept expressing the size of firms relative to an industry effectively serving as an indicator of competition among them. The formula translates a sequence of market shares into a value in the interval [0,1], where 0.0 corresponds to a market with absolute even shares and 1.0 corresponds to a single monopolistic producer.
The index is expressed as

Fig. 4. Influence of context information on betweenness centrality in single topics. For an increasing threshold ϵ, betweenness centrality is obtained. Graphs show violin plots of positions in rankings. The horizontal bars depict positions in classic betweenness ranking. Ranks 8-22 have been removed to enhance readability of the graphs.

where H* is the normalized variant of the original index H, N is the number of firms, and s i the market share of firm i. Data: For obtaining the Herfindahl-Hirshman index for a certain threshold ϵ, we normalize the corresponding histogram of relative betweenness values and feed it to H* . Repeating this for each value of ϵ yields a graph that is depicted in Fig. 6.

Findings:Fig. 6 shows a high monopolistic value for tight constraints, suggesting a corresponding histogram of betweenness values with a single peak only, as well as a decreasing index value for more relaxed constraintswhich approaches a setting of even shares, suggesting a leveled histogram of the betweenness values.

This means that, by quantifying the threshold-specific betweenness values over all topics using the Herfindahl-Hirshman index, the index ultimately reports how a node's embeddedness into the contextual settings of its peers develops.

paragraphs: First, we analogously evaluate the centrality values for all variations of topics and thresholds ϵ for ϵ for RSin Section 4.1.4.

Data: Analogously, we work with individual entries to the topic vector t(s); that is, we obtain closeness centrality for a topic i by using t(s) [i]. Consequently, as a distance function, we use d ctx as specified in Equation 8.

Findings: At 65%, 75%, 85%, and95% quantiles of the ϵ interval, we extract the closeness centrality values for each topic, which results in the four histograms of the Fig. 9. Again, the topic assignments are normalized by their maximum value to fit the interval [0,1] and the closeness values are depicted as values relative to unconstrained closeness centrality (we consider the unconstrained closeness centrality to be derived from structural information alone) ranging within [0,1]. The latter can be obtained in a relative manner as, by definition, the unconstrained values serve as an upper bound to the constrained closeness values (Equation 5).

The first observation in Fig. 9 corresponds to the suggestions obtained from Fig. 8. For tight constraints (i.e., the left-most histogram), there are two topics with some closeness centrality. In addition, for loosened constraints, the centrality values quickly reach 1.0, which corresponds to the centrality value of classical closeness.

Furthermore, we observe a similar trend compared to the corresponding figure depicting betweenness centrality histograms (Fig. 5): Topics with strong assignments -Topics 7, 13, and 20, for exampleexperience a negligible increase in closeness centrality for tighter constraints. In contrast, topics with weak aggregated assignments -Topics 5, 17, and 21, for exampleexperience a rapid increase in closeness centrality for relaxed constraints. In these topics, the values also rapidly level out with the classical closeness values. Fig. 6. Development of betweenness centrality of RSexpressed in terms of Herfindahl-Hirshman index. Interval ϵ ∈ [0.01, 1.0] highlighted.

sections.paper_id: Schoenfeld and Pfeffer - 2024 - Shortest path-based centrality metrics in attributed graphs with node-individual context constraints
paragraphs: As the calculation of closeness centrality directly builds upon the distances to other nodes, we can even more clearly learn about the gap between the topic assignment of a node and those of its peers.

Data: As in Section 4.1.5, we build on the histograms of relative closeness values, normalize them, and feed them to H* to obtain the Herfindahl-Hirshman index for a certain threshold ϵ. Repeating this for each ϵ yields a graph that is depicted in Fig. 8.

Findings: As shown in Fig. 8, the node RSdoes not exhibit a monopolistic structure in the centrality histogram. Rather, the Herfindahl-Hirshman index reports a starting point at around 0.5. In addition, contextual barriers to other nodes are overcome early such that the index rapidly converges to a very low value. This indicates that even for tight constraints, there are two topics in which RSattracts some closeness centrality and that centrality is quite sensitive to loosening the constraints. This implies that the centrality values should quickly converge to the traditional values.

As seen from the two additional profiles added to Fig. 8, there are nodes that might overcome such contextual barriers for even lower thresholds, whereas others maintain a monopolistic structure of the centrality histogram for larger thresholds. The considerable decreases in the index value of the upper gray line for ϵ ≈ 3 suggest that a contextual barrier to an important node has been crossed and a shorter path to the remaining nodes has opened up.Fig. 9.

sections.paper_id: Schoenfeld and Pfeffer - 2024 - Shortest path-based centrality metrics in attributed graphs with node-individual context constraints
paragraphs: The particular combination of structural and contextual information in attributed networks and the derivation of centrality ranking among nodes in such networks has rarely been studied (Schoenfeld & Pfeffer, 2020). In this section, we discuss the findings of our exemplary analysis with related literature.

First, we assume that each node has a unique view on its peers, which is induced by the contextual settings of individuals that influence their perceptioneither functioning like a cognitive filter disregarding some or like an amplifier emphasizing others. We assume that the context information can be exploited to sample a node's unique view. We model this node-individual perspective by sampling a set of shortest paths that remains unique throughout the network and is only valid for a certain reference node. This is where this study contradicts the previous studies. Benyahia & Largeron (2015), for example, extended the classical approach of betweenness centrality by aggregating the weights along shortest paths. They then obtained a betweenness value by following the formula of the classical approach 2. For the case of attributed networks, the resulting centrality values were weighted using the numerical attributes on nodes. They also proposed a variant of closeness centrality that functioned similarly; that is, the edge weights along all shortest paths were aggregated first and a centrality value was obtained using the classic formula. The resulting centrality value was then weighted using the node attributes. While the type of networks (weighted attributed networks with weights associated with the edges as well as numerical attributes on nodes) they considered is similar to ours, their underlying assumption of a globally valid set of shortest paths differs from the proposed approach.

The modeling of the proposed node-individual perspective can also be seen as a form of sampling subgraphs from the network. A similar approach was recently proposed by Chang et al. (2019), which also considers attributed networks with attribute information on nodes. The underlying concept of their framework, however, was to model the influence cascades along paths, which means to let node attributes affect a path sampling. In this study as well, we sample subgraphs but define the reference point differently. Our reference point during sampling remains fixed and is denoted as vertex u in our definition of V Pctx (s, t, u, ϵ) (see Equation 1 above). Consequently, our approach models a node's individual view on the network rather than a notion of influence along a path.

Building on a unique set of shortest paths, we derive a centrality ranking that incorporates node-individual perspectives on a network. In doing so, we propose a novel approach of formulating betweenness and closeness centrality.

Other related studies that operate on similar networks (i.e., possibly weighted networks with attributes on nodes) rather combine traditional centrality metrics with attribute information by obtaining a ranking from traditional algorithms and reordering the resulting ranking using the attribute information.

One of these approaches investigates a scholarly network together with the topic information of publications and was presented by Amjad et al. (2015). Their proposed algorithm, TH Rank, is a variant of Pag-eRank (Page et al., 1999) adapted for a heterogeneous network connecting authors, publications, and outlets using specific types of edges for possible relations (i.e., writing relations (author connected to publications), relations for publishing (outlets connected to publications), and citation relations (author-publication connections). Furthermore, for all three types of nodes, certain topic assignments are derived from a topic model of the publications. Then, TH Rank calculates topic-dependent ranks for authors by considering the varied information determining an author's level of prestige.

In a later study, the same authors proposed an ad-hoc-h index as a node ranking using topic-based citations, basically weighting the h index with topic assignment for authors (Amjad & Daud, 2017). Even though they used network information in the form of a citation graph, their study differs from our approach as they only derived degree information from the network.

A third example of reordering of traditional centrality rankings is an algorithm to minimize the influence of malicious nodes in an infection model. These authors considered networks in which edges were assigned topic probabilities as attributes modeling the strength of a source node u on a target node v with respect to the topic distribution. They proposed a topic-aware betweenness centrality to identify nodes that are important for the diffusion of information in particular topics. However, they first obtained betweenness following the basic approach and then derived a topic-specific value using this centrality value (Yao & Guo, 2015).

Finally, we mention a series of related studies that considered walks instead of paths through a network to obtain node centralities. This is especially interesting for scenarios in which the target network is very large, partially unknown, or only partially specified. Using shortest paths (or even paths) through a network might be unfeasible in such scenarios. Newman (2005), for example, proposed random-walk betweenness to estimate the betweenness scores of nodes building on absorbing random walks. Others sampled subgraphs to effectively estimate centrality considering walks as well, or used a network navigability to estimate walk-based centrality scores (Estrada and Rodríguez-Velázquez, 2005;De Meo et al., 2020).

Our approach to obtain centrality can be adapted to consider walks instead of paths. This is because we subset a given alternating sequence of nodes and edges to obtain a node's individual view on its surroundings. In Equation 1, this is a path P ctx but it can also be a walk instead. One would have to maintain the index of the walks and their respective counterparts under the enforced contextual constraints as both the walks and constraint walks are required as inputs to C Bctx , the betweenness centrality under contextual constraints (Equation 3). For the constraint closeness C Cctx , only the distance matrix induced by constraint walks is required. Further consideration and evaluation of this aspect will be considered in the future.

sections.paper_id: Schoenfeld and Pfeffer - 2024 - Shortest path-based centrality metrics in attributed graphs with node-individual context constraints
paragraphs: We present an approach to integrate the context information of attributed networks into shortest path-based centrality metrics and evaluate this approach using node attributes. To co-integrate both structural and attribute information, we assume that the attribute information influences path traversal during the collection of shortest paths and inhibits the traversal as soon as attribute information relevant for a path diverges more than a predefined threshold from a reference context. The reference context refers to the vertex for which centrality metrics are obtained and hence is unique for each calculation of centrality values. This induces a node-individual view into the centrality metrics that can be interpreted in terms of node embeddedness; that is, our evaluation shows how compatible a node is to its surroundings.

An important argument against using other modeling approaches (e. g., multilayer networks) lies in our distance function. We demonstrate the use of a distance function that does not satisfy the triangle inequality, which makes edge traversal during the collection of shortest paths depend on the reference node. Modeling this node-individual perspective on a network using a multilayer network would require |V| × k different layers for each threshold value ϵ.

We further show how the influence of context information can be adjusted by selecting different thresholds for a comparison of contexts. More appealing than the single threshold values is the development of increasing thresholds, which we visualize using the Herfindahl-Hirshman index. In our case study, this index expresses whether a reference node represents a rather unique set of interests with low compatibility to its peers or whether it is well-embedded into a group of peers with similar interests.

In this case study, we use aggregated assignments derived from a topic model of scientific papers as node attributes. However, our approach can very well be generalized to any attribute on nodes. Therefore, an appropriate distance function is required that supports a comparison of such attributes.

Future work needs to consider other types of attributes that would better capture the dynamic and often chaotic nature of the context information. Examples include dynamic or complex attributes that, ultimately, create additional analytic opportunities and can help to gain a more detailed understanding of complex social networks.
